title: We've Got Spirit

jokes.text: Nick delivering deep philosophical ramblings about mortality and meaning, then Lindsay flatly asks 'Are you stoned?' and he laughs 'A little bit.'
jokes.setup: Nick rambles earnestly about death, John Bonham, the meaning of life — framing himself as a sensitive, deep thinker who really connects with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's flat 'Are you stoned?' instantly deflates all the pseudo-profundity; his cheerful admission confirms it
jokes.explanation: Classic Freaks and Geeks deflation move: the show lets Nick go full 'sensitive stoner philosopher' for 45 seconds before Lindsay's one-line reality check lands perfectly. Works because it's character-specific (Nick IS this guy) and Lindsay's deadpan is the audience surrogate. The structure is tight — long windup, short punch.

jokes.text: Nick, mid-existential ramble, pivots to asking where John Bonham is right now — 'Is he in the ground rotting away somewhere, or maybe he's up in heaven partying with Jimi and Janis?'
jokes.setup: Nick is supposedly having a deep conversation about life and death
jokes.punchline: His heaven concept is specifically a rock-star party — the 'heavy' question is really just a stoner's concert fantasy
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'partying with Jimi and Janis' is what elevates this above a generic stoner joke. It's funny because it reveals exactly how Nick's brain works — his conception of the afterlife is essentially a VIP room. Good character comedy but secondary to the 'are you stoned' punchline.

jokes.text: Daniel rants that jocks 'think they're such badasses, walking around like they cured cancer. They're just monkeys.' Then immediately does a monkey impression.
jokes.setup: Daniel is mounting a serious philosophical critique of jock culture
jokes.punchline: He undercuts his own argument by immediately doing chimp sounds, which is funnier and more juvenile than anything the jocks did
jokes.explanation: Works as self-undermining character comedy — Daniel's mock-intellectual disdain collapses into the exact kind of goofing around he's criticizing. The monkey impression is the punchline the monologue sets up without realizing it. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ken: 'Where's Lover Boy?' about Nick. Then after being corrected: 'I thought you two were joined at the tongue or something.'
jokes.setup: Nick isn't present; Ken needs to know where Lindsay's boyfriend is
jokes.punchline: 'Joined at the tongue' — Ken's trademark acidic delivery of a gross image to describe affection
jokes.explanation: Ken's sarcasm is always tight and this is a clean example — the phrase is slightly more anatomically specific than expected, which is his voice. Not a standout but consistent character work.

jokes.text: Daniel asks 'Are you pregnant?' when Lindsay pulls Kim aside. Then Kim does it again privately to Lindsay right after.
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she needs to talk to Kim privately
jokes.punchline: Both Daniel and then Kim separately jump straight to 'are you pregnant?' — revealing this is just how these people think
jokes.explanation: The double-beat makes this work — it would be a mediocre one-liner if only Daniel said it, but Kim immediately repeating it privately is the real joke. It reveals something true about their social world without comment. The structure earns it.

jokes.text: Kim privately asks Lindsay 'So... are you pregnant?' again — now alone, same question
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just dismissed the pregnancy question publicly as ridiculous
jokes.punchline: Alone with Lindsay, Kim immediately asks again — suggesting she truly believes it's possible
jokes.explanation: The callback within 30 seconds makes the joke better — Kim's public 'fine, I'll drop it' was entirely performative. The repetition reveals that Kim actually thought the pregnancy theory was plausible and was just being socially strategic. Character comedy at its best: the second instance tells you more about Kim than the first.

jokes.text: Kim's advice on how to handle Nick: 'Let me straighten him out. I'll beat some sense into him.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay confides she wants to break up with Nick because he's too intense
jokes.punchline: Kim's solution is literally to beat him, delivered as if this is helpful and reasonable
jokes.explanation: Purely character comedy — this is only funny because it's Kim, who absolutely would do this and presents it as a reasonable relationship intervention. The deadpan logic of 'I'll beat some sense into him, it'll be fine' is perfectly Kim.

jokes.text: Cindy tells Sam about her feelings for Todd: 'It's not like a crush. It's like...an obsession.'
jokes.setup: Sam asks if Cindy still has a crush on Todd after the locker-decoration awkwardness
jokes.punchline: She earnestly self-corrects 'crush' to 'obsession' as if this is a clarification that makes things better, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Short, clean, and Cindy-specific. The word swap from 'crush' to 'obsession' is the entire joke — the escalation upward delivered as a casual clarification. Economy is excellent. Works on rewatch because we know how oblivious Cindy is to Sam's feelings.

jokes.text: Sam complaining about the school mascot: 'The mascot is depressing me. Look at him. He's running in place. What is that? Is that supposed to be funny?' — while the mascot runs in place trying to generate crowd energy
jokes.setup: The assembly is meant to pump up the school; the mascot is doing his best
jokes.punchline: Sam's flat, detailed criticism of the mascot's physical comedy is more entertaining than the mascot himself
jokes.explanation: Works as setup for the mascot subplot — Sam critiques the mascot while clearly watching him with professional envy (he'll become the mascot). The irony is light but functional. Gains rewatch value once we know the arc.

jokes.text: Bill's final complaint about the mascot: 'I wish he'd sit down. He's blocking Cindy.'
jokes.setup: Bill seems to be delivering a genuine critique of the mascot's performance quality
jokes.punchline: He's actually just annoyed because the mascot is obstructing his view of his crush — the 'critique' was self-interested all along
jokes.explanation: This is the best Bill joke in the episode. In four words ('He's blocking Cindy') the show reveals that Bill's entire earnest critique was actually just a teenager's grievance about not being able to see a girl. The misdirection is perfectly executed — his analytical tone never changes, which makes the reveal funnier. Very close to an 8 in craft: the economy is exceptional and it's unmistakably Bill's voice.

jokes.text: Neal on cheerleaders dating athletes: 'Cheerleaders have to date athletes. It's the law.'
jokes.setup: Sam asks why Cindy likes Todd given that he's clearly a jerk to her
jokes.punchline: Neal's flat, authoritative pronouncement treats high school social hierarchy as jurisprudence
jokes.explanation: Strong economy and Neal-specific delivery. The joke works because 'it's the law' transforms an unspoken social rule into something absurdly codified. Neal's character is defined by this kind of confident, slightly-off authority. Genuinely quotable.

jokes.text: Sam watches the mascot attempt a fall and mutters: 'He can't even fall funny.'
jokes.setup: The mascot tries physical comedy at the assembly
jokes.punchline: Sam's clinical critique — 'he can't even fall funny' — is the funniest thing about the mascot's performance
jokes.explanation: Another tight economy joke from Sam. 'Can't even fall funny' is a specific, expert-sounding critique from a 14-year-old with no credentials. Gets rewatch value as Sam will later become the mascot and has this conversation with himself implicitly.

jokes.text: One kid tells another 'I heard that Stevie Wonder was Jewish' — the other corrects 'You're thinking of Sammy Davis Jr.' — first kid insists: 'No. I read it in the newspaper.'
jokes.setup: Background conversation during the aftermath of the mascot's injury
jokes.punchline: The correction is wrong too (Sammy Davis Jr. converted to Judaism — so one is accidentally right), but the first kid citing 'I read it in the newspaper' as authoritative on completely wrong information is the real gag
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Freaks and Geeks background comedy. The joke operates on multiple levels: both kids are confidently wrong, the non-sequitur timing is perfect (happens during an injury crisis), and the first kid's 'I read it in the newspaper' adds mock-authority to misinformation. The show trusts the audience to find this without underlining it.

jokes.text: Neal's 'cousin slipped into a coma once. When he woke up, he spoke fluent Spanish' anecdote, delivered casually while discussing the mascot's concussion.
jokes.setup: Cindy explains the mascot can't sleep because his mom fears he'll slip into a coma
jokes.punchline: Neal offers this as if it's a helpful, relevant data point — his cousin woke from a coma speaking Spanish, which neither helps nor makes sense
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Neal delivers it with complete confidence as a conversational contribution, and nobody addresses it. The absurdity of 'woke up speaking fluent Spanish' is perfectly unexplained — Neal offers no commentary, which is funnier than any explanation would be. Strong economy and very Neal-coded.

jokes.text: Bill's 'subliminal message' theory: 'She wants you to be the new mascot. I really need a mascot. I really need SAM to be the new mascot.' Repeating it three times as if translating.
jokes.setup: Cindy mentions they need a new mascot and leaves; Bill turns to Sam
jokes.punchline: Bill earnestly decodes Cindy's comment as a 'subliminal message' aimed at Sam specifically — his repetition of the 'translation' three times with escalating smugness is the joke
jokes.explanation: Bill's wingman logic is endearingly delusional. The joke is his absolute conviction that an indirect public statement was a coded personal invitation. The triple repetition with increasing specificity is structured comedy — it's not just misreading, it's elaborate misreading.

jokes.text: Neal announces he's going to be the new Norseman mascot; Bill immediately counters 'No. Sam's gonna be the new Norseman.' 'But Sam's not funny.' Sam: 'Oh yeah, I am.'
jokes.setup: The three geeks are debating who should try out for mascot
jokes.punchline: Neal's dismissal of Sam ('he's not funny') delivered with zero malice — just matter-of-fact evaluation of a friend — and Sam's indignant, unconvincing defense
jokes.explanation: Good ensemble dynamic — Neal's clinical honesty is not mean-spirited, which makes it funnier. The irony is that Neal will actually end up being the funny mascot, so this is accidental foreshadowing.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Being the school mascot has always been my dream.' Sam: 'Yeah, since lunch.'
jokes.setup: Neal pleads for the mascot role with emotional investment
jokes.punchline: Sam's perfect callback to the fact that Neal only just decided this was his dream moments ago at lunch
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest lines in the episode. Three words ('Yeah, since lunch') completely deflate Neal's manufactured passion without cruelty. The economy is exceptional — Sam could have said 'you just decided that at lunch' but 'Yeah, since lunch' is funnier by a mile. Highly quotable, well-structured, and perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Sam invokes the Star Wars 'Help me Obi-Wan Kenobi' line to convince Neal, and Neal caves immediately: 'You had to go and quote Star Wars, didn't you?'
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to get Neal to let him have the mascot tryout
jokes.punchline: The Star Wars reference as a social/emotional trump card — and Neal's weary acknowledgment that it worked — reveals exactly what kind of nerds these are
jokes.explanation: The 'you had to go and quote Star Wars' line is the real punch — Neal knows he's being manipulated and submits anyway because the manipulation is too on-point. Captures the geek social contract perfectly. Strong character integration for both.

jokes.text: Harold lists his community credentials to justify going to the game: 'I'm a member of the Rotary. And the Masons. And Councilman Applebee just last week asked me to run for the school board.' Then: 'Boy, I could be the mayor, I wouldn't get any respect around this house.'
jokes.setup: Harold is trying to explain why he must attend the basketball game as a 'community leader'
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of credentials — Rotary, Masons, school board — building to the self-pitying 'I could be the mayor' is the payoff; the family clearly does not care
jokes.explanation: Harold Weir is one of TV's great underappreciated comic characters, and this is a perfect showcase. The escalation from Rotary Club to hypothetical mayor is exactly how Harold thinks — grandiose self-perception vs. zero family respect. 'Boy, I could be the mayor' has become one of the show's hallmark Harold moments.

jokes.text: Bill informs Sam: 'I heard the mascot has to change with the cheerleaders in the girls' locker room.' Sam's response: 'Really? That doesn't sound right.'
jokes.setup: Sam just got the mascot role; Bill delivers this 'information'
jokes.punchline: Sam's immediate skepticism ('That doesn't sound right') is the punchline — he's 14 and smart enough to know this is too good to be true, but clearly part of him wants to believe
jokes.explanation: Standard teenage-wish-fulfillment joke, but Sam's measured skepticism saves it from being pure lowbrow. The line 'that doesn't sound right' is funnier than a reaction of excitement would be.

jokes.text: After Cindy vaguely encourages Sam at auditions, Sam drily asks Bill: 'You think there's a subliminal message in there somewhere?' — Bill: 'For your sake, I hope not.'
jokes.setup: Callback to Bill's earlier 'subliminal message' theory; this time Cindy was clearly NOT giving Sam a signal
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'For your sake, I hope not' — a sudden reversal where the wingman advises against his own strategy
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. Bill is now using his own previously-established framework against itself — 'for your sake, I hope not' acknowledges that Cindy's last statement was NOT encouraging, while also undercutting his earlier confidence. Five words that do a lot of work. High economy.

jokes.text: One of the cheerleaders on the old mascot: 'That guy was as funny as a car wreck.' — then immediately catches herself: 'I was just kidding.'
jokes.setup: The cheerleader is comparing Sam favorably to the old mascot who just broke his arm
jokes.punchline: The 'car wreck' simile is accidentally dark given that the kid is in the hospital; her self-correction makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Small but effective dark comedy beat. The self-correction lands better than the original line — 'I was just kidding' doesn't help and she knows it. The show treats this as throwaway, which is the right call.

jokes.text: Someone notes that Neal is 'better than the old mascot. That guy was as funny as a car wreck.' The injured mascot's friend is present and protests. Person: 'I was just kidding.'
jokes.setup: The injured mascot's friend Herbert is standing right there
jokes.punchline: The 'car wreck' simile lands just as the injured party's connection becomes apparent — a moment of accidental cruelty immediately walked back
jokes.explanation: A light cringe-comedy beat. The 'car wreck' comparison to someone who just had a physical accident with a head injury is the joke, compounded by the immediate awkward retraction. Functional but not memorable.

jokes.text: Herbert, the injured mascot, tells Sam to pull out a nose hair if he starts falling asleep — advice delivered while Herbert himself hasn't slept in days and is visibly struggling
jokes.setup: Herbert can't sleep because of his concussion; someone asks if he fell asleep last night
jokes.punchline: Herbert immediately pivots to giving Sam practical advice on staying awake — as if he's an expert, when he's the one in crisis
jokes.explanation: Herbert is a minor running gag throughout the episode — his post-concussion state providing dark background comedy. This beat is functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: The cheerleader suggests Sam try out for the musical as 'a dancing Yeshiva student' in Fiddler on the Roof, seemingly as a genuine compliment after his mascot performance.
jokes.setup: Sam has just shown impressive dancing ability
jokes.punchline: Her earnest recommendation is casually, obliviously ethnically specific — 'a dancing Yeshiva student' is clearly based on Sam's appearance, delivered without any self-awareness
jokes.explanation: A small, well-observed joke about casual obliviousness. The show doesn't comment on it — the humor is in the cheerleader's utter sincerity.

jokes.text: The loser at mascot tryouts shouts: 'It's all politics!'
jokes.setup: The head cheerleader announces Sam as the new mascot
jokes.punchline: A competing kid cries 'politics' about a high school mascot audition — treating it with the gravity of a rigged election
jokes.explanation: Quick background joke — the word 'politics' applied to a mascot audition is the entire gag. Economy is its only real virtue.

jokes.text: The school chant: 'Assassinate Lincoln!' — the pep rally crowd screams it enthusiastically
jokes.setup: McKinley school is rallying against their rival Lincoln High
jokes.punchline: The chant is technically about Lincoln High School but the words are historically horrifying — 'assassinate Lincoln' delivered as cheerful school spirit
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes and probably the darkest. The show named one school after McKinley (also assassinated) and the rival Lincoln — then lets the crowd cheerfully chant 'Assassinate Lincoln' as pure school spirit. Nobody acknowledges the double meaning. The joke is entirely in the staging and the audience recognition. Highly original, maximum economy, gets funnier on rewatch when you notice the McKinley detail too.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'This place is turning into a cult. These jocks, they put a ball through a hoop — whoop-de-freakin'-do.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is hyped about the game; Daniel is conspicuously unimpressed
jokes.punchline: 'Whoop-de-freakin'-do' — Daniel's sardonic dismissal of athletic achievement delivered while surrounded by genuine enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Standard Daniel voice — appropriately cynical but not exceptional. 'Whoop-de-freakin'-do' is the kind of line Daniel exists to deliver.

jokes.text: Ken reveals he's going to the game because: 'I heard we're gonna lose, right? I kinda think it's funny when the jocks cry. "Ooooh, they cheated!"' — then does a mock-crying jock impression
jokes.setup: The freaks are all ostensibly opposed to school spirit; Ken announces he's going
jokes.punchline: His motivation for attending is schadenfreude — he's going specifically to enjoy jock tears, doing a perfect impression of a crying jock
jokes.explanation: Ken's motivation being pure sadism is perfect character work. The impression — 'Ooooh, they cheated!' — sells it. Also gains rewatch value because McKinley actually wins, denying Ken his anticipated pleasure.

jokes.text: Nick appears at the freaks' hangout; Ken's sotto voce warning to Lindsay: 'Don't do it. Please.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to work up the nerve to break up with Nick; Nick arrives suddenly
jokes.punchline: Ken's whispered 'Don't do it. Please.' — which could mean don't break up with him OR don't make a scene, but either way is Ken's version of emotional support
jokes.explanation: Ken's delivery makes this — four words of desperate whispered pleading from the most sardonic character in the show is inherently funny. The ambiguity (don't break up with him / don't cause drama) is a bonus.

jokes.text: 'I'd hate to be that guy's drum set tonight.' — Ken's parting shot as Nick and Lindsay leave together
jokes.setup: Nick has clearly picked up on the tension; he and Lindsay leave
jokes.punchline: Ken imagining Nick taking out emotional distress on his drum kit — simultaneously funny and a bit ominous given Nick's established instability
jokes.explanation: One of Ken's best lines in the episode. The specificity of empathizing with AN INANIMATE OBJECT (the drum set) rather than Nick or Lindsay is Ken's entire personality distilled. Dark, economical, perfectly delivered. Gets funnier in retrospect because Nick does eventually channel his feelings into his music.

jokes.text: Daniel, watching Lindsay leave with Nick: 'I'd hate to be that guy's drum set tonight.'
jokes.setup: The group watches Nick and Lindsay drive away, Nick presumably emotional
jokes.punchline: Daniel's only concern is the fate of the drum kit — the inanimate object as victim of Nick's emotional volatility
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. Daniel's line is pure poetry in its economy — one sentence that: (1) captures Nick's emotional volatility, (2) reveals that everyone around Nick knows how he processes feelings, (3) is deeply character-specific (Daniel would think in terms of the drum kit, not Nick's emotional state), and (4) is genuinely quotable. 'I'd hate to be that guy's drum set tonight' works as a complete comedy-tragedy statement about Nick without ever mentioning Nick's feelings. It justifies 8+ because it's doing more work per word than almost any other line in the episode, and the 'drum set' as surrogate victim is genuinely original.

jokes.text: Herbert, injured ex-mascot, gives Sam elaborate warnings about climbing the pyramid: 'Be careful not to pull their hair, 'cause they'll jerk up and you'll fall. Be careful not to pull their bra straps, 'cause they'll wiggle and you'll fall.' Vicki: 'HERBERT! Do you mind?'
jokes.setup: Sam is about to try the pyramid for the first time; Herbert offers advice
jokes.punchline: Herbert's advice is extremely specific about cheerleaders' bodies — the advice of a kid who has clearly learned these lessons through painful personal experience — Vicki shuts him down
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy here is that Herbert's advice is both inappropriate AND clearly experiential — he knows exactly what happens because he did exactly this. Vicki's 'Herbert, do you mind?' is the punchline delivered as scene-cutting authority.

jokes.text: Kim: 'That better have been water. That's all I'm sayin.'
jokes.setup: Kim is soaked from the water balloon
jokes.punchline: Kim's barely-contained threat implies she's considered worse possibilities for the balloon's contents — the horror of the alternative is the joke
jokes.explanation: Kim's menacing understatement — 'That's all I'm sayin'' — carries the whole threat in the implication. The line works because Kim is exactly the person who would think of what else could have been in that balloon. Very Kim: she's horrified but also has encyclopedic knowledge of possible humiliations.

jokes.text: Jean answers the phone for Nick, mouths 'I'm not here' to Lindsay; Jean then tells Nick Lindsay is 'out with her father...I don't know what they're doing, but they're out there doing it.' Then: 'OK. I'll let her know you called. Bye-bye, sweetie.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay doesn't want to talk to Nick; Jean agrees to cover
jokes.punchline: Jean's cover story is hilariously bad — the suspicious 'out there doing it,' the overly familiar 'bye-bye, sweetie' to her daughter's boyfriend she's never met
jokes.explanation: Jean's earnest attempt at deception is perfectly executed. 'They're out there doing it' is accidentally suggestive, and 'bye-bye, sweetie' to Nick transforms Jean into an inadvertent Nick enabler. Strongly Jean-coded — only she would both agree to lie AND make such a mess of it.

jokes.text: Jean: 'Lindsay, I don't like to lie.' — her immediate guilty disclosure after hanging up
jokes.setup: Jean just awkwardly lied for Lindsay on the phone
jokes.punchline: Her statement of principle comes after the lie is already done — technically true, morally useless
jokes.explanation: Five words that capture Jean completely — she's the kind of person who will do the thing she disapproves of and then immediately announce she disapproves of it. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: The phone rings (presumably a hang-up or prank call); Harold answers and delivers an escalating angry monologue: 'Hello. Hello? Ken, if this is you, be here an hour ago... I don't like having to wait around... I got other stuff to do. Are you gonna answer me, KEENNN?... Hello—' [hangs up]
jokes.setup: Phone rings; Harold answers expecting Ken
jokes.punchline: Harold's escalating aggression toward a silent phone line — from polite to frustrated to shouting 'KEENNN' to abruptly hanging up — with nobody on the line (or Nick, who just listened to a rant about Ken)
jokes.explanation: Harold's paranoid assumption that every unexplained event is Ken's fault is great character comedy. The escalation from 'Hello?' to 'KEENNN?' is perfectly Harold. On rewatch we realize it was likely Nick — making Harold's rant a bizarre accidental message to his daughter's soon-to-be-ex.

jokes.text: After the first mysterious call, Harold gets another and warns: 'Fine. If this isn't Ken, you better not call back here...or I'll find you.'
jokes.setup: Callback to the first mysterious phone call; Harold is now primed
jokes.punchline: 'Or I'll find you' — Harold's threat is specifically menacing in a completely suburban way; the implicit threat of a middle-aged man finding an anonymous caller is absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Or I'll find you' is a disproportionate threat delivered with complete Weir-family earnestness. The callback structure works — Harold has been building to this, and the escalation from confused to menacing is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: Jean wakes Harold: 'I just had a conversation with Lindsay about her love life... I feel like...a mother.' Harold: 'Yeah. You're on my leg.' Jean: 'Oh, oh, I'm sorry. I just—I'm so tickled!'
jokes.setup: Jean bursts in to share her breakthrough moment of maternal connection with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Harold's grounded domestic observation ('You're on my leg') undercuts Jean's emotional peak perfectly; Jean apologizes and immediately resumes being 'tickled'
jokes.explanation: Beautiful Weir parents scene. Jean's emotional crescendo ('I feel like...a mother') meets Harold's physical reality ('You're on my leg') — and then Jean's ability to absorb the deflation and remain 'tickled' is perfect. Both characters at their most themselves. The domestic specificity ('you're on my leg') is what makes it — not a generic deflation but an exact, boring, real detail.

jokes.text: Harold suggests Lindsay tell Nick 'he's a greasebucket and you deserve better' — Lindsay reacts with horror.
jokes.setup: Jean and Harold are giving Lindsay advice on how to break up with Nick
jokes.punchline: 'Greasebucket' — Harold's specific insult is dated, oddly specific, and exactly what a 1950s-mentality dad would say
jokes.explanation: 'Greasebucket' is a fantastic word choice — it's Harold-specific, period-specific, and absolutely the kind of thing he'd say. The show is set in 1980 but Harold already seems like he's 30 years behind. Highly quotable for this episode.

jokes.text: Sam tells Harold he's sore from mascot practice. Then: 'I think Cindy Sanders likes me.' Harold: 'Oh yeah?' Sam: 'Yeah. I got to form a human pyramid on top of her.' Harold: 'Well, that'll do it.'
jokes.setup: Sam shares his romantic intelligence with his dad
jokes.punchline: Harold's 'well, that'll do it' — validating Sam's pyramid-as-courtship theory with the pragmatic acceptance of a dad who doesn't want to unpack this
jokes.explanation: Harold's 'that'll do it' is perfectly calibrated dad comedy — he registers the absurdity, chooses not to engage, and provides noncommittal encouragement. The delivery would be entirely straight-faced, which is funnier. Classic Freaks and Geeks father-son dynamic.

jokes.text: Daniel's relationship philosophy: 'Dumbest thing you can do — let a girl know how much you like her. Then they just run.' Kim: 'Oh, is that why you're such a bastard to me?' Daniel: 'Yeah. Well, you're still here, aren't ya?'
jokes.setup: Daniel is opining on why Nick's relationships fail
jokes.punchline: Kim's blunt application of Daniel's theory to their own relationship — 'is that why you're such a bastard to me?' — and Daniel's complete, cheerful confirmation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. Daniel's terrible-but-confident relationship advice is funny on its own, but Kim's immediate 'is that why you're such a bastard to me?' is the real setup — and Daniel's 'Yeah. Well, you're still here, aren't ya?' is the perfect punchline. It's dark, it's true to both characters, and it reveals their whole relationship in two lines. Daniel's confirmation is delivered without guilt, which makes it worse and funnier. Strong 8+ justification: character integration is essentially perfect, the structure is textbook setup-misdirect-punch, and the dark self-awareness elevates it above cheap.

jokes.text: Kim spots the Lincoln car that water-ballooned her and immediately demands pursuit: 'Those jocks from Lincoln that nailed us, that's them! Go. Go. Go. Go. Before they get away!'
jokes.setup: The freaks are driving normally when Kim spots their assailants
jokes.punchline: Kim's instantaneous tactical response — 'Go! Go! Go!' — delivered like a military operation over a water balloon incident
jokes.explanation: Kim's disproportionate tactical urgency over a water balloon is very her. The callback works; her anger has been simmering and she mobilizes instantly. 'Before they get away' implies a heist-level stakes assessment.

jokes.text: Neal watching Sam in mascot practice commentary: 'You see that phony sword? That is a great prop. Sam's not even using it. What do you do with a phony sword? A million things. You fall on it. You stab yourself. You stab the other mascot.' Bill: 'That's not funny.' Neal: 'It is if you do it right.'
jokes.setup: Neal is critiquing Sam's mascot performance from the bleachers with the intensity of a comedy showrunner
jokes.punchline: 'It is if you do it right' — Neal's defense of mascot-stabbing-comedy as a legitimate craft, delivered with absolute authority
jokes.explanation: Neal as armchair comedy director is a perfect use of his character. The escalating list (fall on it, stab yourself, stab the OTHER mascot) followed by Bill's reasonable objection and Neal's 'it is if you do it right' is beautifully structured. Neal's insistence that dark mascot comedy is valid is inherently funny. Gets rewatch value because Neal does eventually perform many of these bits.

jokes.text: Neal demonstrates proper Norseman comedy for Bill — 'A Norseman in a chorus line' and 'Hula dancer Norseman. Yes. A Norseman in Hawaii. That's funny!'
jokes.setup: Neal is teaching Sam how to be funny as the mascot (Sam is not present)
jokes.punchline: Neal performing these routines at full intensity for an audience of one Bill, completely unselfconsciously — in public
jokes.explanation: The funniest element is the commitment — Neal doing a full Hula-Norseman routine in public while narrating his own comedy theory. Bill's 'I can't be seen with you' is the implied reaction beat. Works primarily as visual/physical comedy inferred from the dialogue.

jokes.text: Bill: 'I can't be seen with you.' — reacting to Neal's public mascot demonstration
jokes.setup: Neal is performing elaborate mascot comedy bits in public with full commitment
jokes.punchline: Bill — himself a member of the school's least cool group — disowning Neal for being too embarrassing
jokes.explanation: The irony of Bill — who has almost no social capital — being embarrassed by Neal is the whole joke. Economy is perfect. Works as a character beat as much as a joke.

jokes.text: Nick's account of the Heidi Henderson incident: 'I wrote her poems...lots and lots of poems.' Then after she started showing them around: 'I want to get those poems back, right? And her dad catches me, and he starts yelling at me. And then the cops came. The whole thing just got blown totally out of proportion.'
jokes.setup: Nick is explaining his history with Heidi — apparently a stalking incident — to Lindsay, trying to preempt her breaking up with him
jokes.punchline: 'The whole thing just got blown totally out of proportion' — Nick's sincere belief that breaking into an ex's house to retrieve poems is proportionate and the COPS coming is the overreaction
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best character-comedy moment. Nick's self-narrative is pitch-perfect — he describes what is objectively a stalking incident ('lots and lots of poems,' breaking in to retrieve them, fighting with the dad) and arrives at 'the whole thing got blown totally out of proportion.' The joke is that Nick has ZERO awareness of how this sounds. The 'lots and lots of poems' is a perfect detail — not just poems, LOTS AND LOTS of them. Character integration is essentially 10: no other character on the show could deliver this. The dramatic irony (Lindsay is about to break up with him, Nick is trying to reassure her, and he's actually terrifying her more) adds a layer of cringe. Justifies the 8+ score: comparable quality to the 8.0 anchor, with stronger character specificity and dramatic irony.

jokes.text: After Nick's entire stalking confession, Lindsay says: 'Nick, I would never show your poems to anyone.' Nick: '[Laughs] You rule...so hard.' And then: 'Let's go to the game.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just heard a story that should make her want to run; she finds the one reassuring thing to say
jokes.punchline: Nick's relief is total and immediate — 'you rule so hard' — and he pivots to 'let's go to the game' as if everything is normal; Lindsay is now trapped by her own kindness
jokes.explanation: The cringe here is that Lindsay's attempt to be kind has now un-broken-up-with Nick. 'You rule so hard' is perfectly Nick — excessive, earnest, slightly off. The immediate 'let's go to the game' is the comic beat: he's processed and moved on in five seconds.

jokes.text: Sam — hopelessly in love with Cindy — advises her: 'Cindy, he probably has a lot of other girlfriends.' Then: 'I just don't want you to get too hung up on him just in case, you know, he won't go out with you or something.'
jokes.setup: Cindy is excited that Todd might ask her out; Sam is sabotaging Todd out of jealousy, disguised as concern
jokes.punchline: Sam's 'concern' is transparently self-interested — he's the least subtle saboteur, warning Cindy off Todd while she just got kissed by Todd
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy — Sam is so transparently self-serving in his 'concern' that the audience winces. The second line ('just in case he won't go out with you or something') is the more specific joke — Sam is covering his jealousy with poorly executed protective-friend framing.

jokes.text: Kim and Daniel preparing to spray-paint a Lincoln kid's car. Kim: 'I saw 'em. Do it.' Daniel: 'Why don't you do it?' Kim: 'Cause I'm a girl.'
jokes.setup: The freaks are about to vandalize a car in retaliation for the water balloon incident
jokes.punchline: Kim — who is clearly the most aggressive and least rule-abiding member of the group — invokes being 'a girl' as an excuse not to spray paint the car
jokes.explanation: The hypocrisy is the entire joke: Kim, who suggested the vandalism, organized the retaliation, and will absolutely fight anyone — invoking gender as an excuse for this specific crime is perfectly Kim. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Kim wants the boys to vandalize the Lincoln car. Daniel and Ken debate who should do it. Kim: 'Why don't you do it?' Ken: ''Cause I'm a girl.'
jokes.setup: Kim wants revenge; the boys are reluctant to act
jokes.punchline: Ken's explanation for not participating: 'Cause I'm a girl' — a completely straight-faced, illogical excuse
jokes.explanation: One of the best Ken lines in the episode. 'Cause I'm a girl' is delivered with complete straight-faced deadpan — Ken is not a girl, but the logic he's applying (Kim issued the order, Kim should execute it) somehow comes out as a gender claim. The absurdity of the line, combined with Ken's total confidence, makes it land perfectly. Economy is exceptional — four words, very big laugh. Justifies near-8 because the non-sequitur is original, economical, and unmistakably Ken's voice.

jokes.text: The Lincoln kid squares up to Daniel: 'What are you gonna do about it, huh?' — Daniel's response: 'Tell my mother that I love her.'
jokes.setup: The Lincoln guys have caught the freaks spray-painting their car; confrontation
jokes.punchline: Daniel, facing a fight, completely abandons any pretense of toughness and asks for a message to be passed to his mother
jokes.explanation: The best single joke in the episode. Daniel, who has been posturing as a tough guy throughout — mocking jocks, agreeing to vandalize cars, acting like he'll handle whatever comes — completely folds. 'Tell my mother that I love her' is a perfect subversion: it's the exact wrong response to a tough-guy challenge, delivered with the sincerity of a soldier's last words. It's funny because of everything we know about Daniel (false bravado collapsing instantly), the economy is perfect (seven words), and it is absolutely quotable. Justifies the 8.3+: it matches the 8.0 anchor in quality and arguably exceeds it in character specificity and originality of the specific deflation image.

jokes.text: Sam puts on the Norseman mascot head and stumbles around completely blind — bumping into people, having no spatial awareness, to a series of increasingly irritated reactions
jokes.setup: Vicki tells Sam to put on the head and walk around to get used to it
jokes.punchline: The head provides zero visibility and Sam becomes a danger to everyone around him, including the person who just told him to do this
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup — the oversized mascot head making the wearer helpless is a reliable gag. Effective but not particularly original. Works on screen more than in transcript form.

jokes.text: Sam in the Norseman head tries to high-five someone — gives them a poke in the face instead, completely disoriented
jokes.setup: A player calls for a high-five from the mascot
jokes.punchline: Sam, unable to see anything, connects with the person's face instead
jokes.explanation: Functional slapstick. Predictable once the can't-see-in-the-head setup is established, but executed cleanly. Lower score because it's the expected payoff of a setup rather than a surprise.

jokes.text: Neal inspires Sam to go out as mascot by invoking Steve Martin: 'You think Steve Martin always feels like being funny? No, but he goes out on that stage every night with his arrow and his banjo, and he kills.'
jokes.setup: Sam is refusing to perform because he's upset about Cindy
jokes.punchline: Neal's pep talk invokes Steve Martin as the platonic ideal of professionalism — the arrow and the banjo as specific, beloved props — delivered with complete sincerity as motivational wisdom
jokes.explanation: Neal's Steve Martin reference is a perfect encapsulation of who these kids are — geeks who know comedy theory but don't know social grace. 'With his arrow and his banjo, and he kills' is specific enough (it's accurate to late-70s/80s Steve Martin) that it's funny as an observation AND as character revelation. Neal drawing a direct line between Steve Martin and a high school mascot is the joke. High quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: Harold introduces Lindsay's beaten-up, just-vandalized freak friends to Councilman Appleby, Stan Miller, and the Sheriff, then awkwardly waves them off: 'Why don't you just run along with your friends now, OK?' — then to the community pillars: 'Teenagers. What are you gonna do?'
jokes.setup: Harold has just introduced Lindsay to his most respectable friends; her vandal friends show up visibly beaten up
jokes.punchline: 'Teenagers. What are you gonna do?' — Harold's mortified deflection delivered as a resigned universal truth to a sheriff and a councilman
jokes.explanation: Harold's social humiliation in front of his 'pillars of the community' friends is perfectly staged cringe. The specific inclusion of a SHERIFF makes it worse — Harold is trying to be respectable to law enforcement while his daughter's friends are visibly post-vandalism. 'Teenagers, what are you gonna do?' is Harold's survival mechanism: reframing catastrophe as universal wisdom.

jokes.text: Jean intercepts Nick at the game, tells him 'Lindsay really does care about you. She told me so last night. She just feels overwhelmed right now. She needs her space.' — Nick's face falls; he confirms Lindsay didn't actually break up with him yet
jokes.setup: Jean is trying to be helpful; she doesn't know Lindsay hasn't broken up with Nick yet
jokes.punchline: Jean has just essentially pre-broken-up with Nick for Lindsay — badly — while also revealing Lindsay's private feelings, becoming an inadvertent relationship saboteur
jokes.explanation: Jean's well-intentioned meddling is a running thread that pays off here. The joke is structural — Jean has now meddled twice in Lindsay's love life, both times with opposite effects (first encouraging Lindsay to break up, now soft-breaking-up on her behalf while Lindsay hasn't done it yet). Nick's stunned reaction completes the cringe.

jokes.text: Lindsay confronts Jean: 'Mom, did you break up with my boyfriend?' Jean: 'I don't — no, I just, uh — oh my goodness.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just found out Jean talked to Nick
jokes.punchline: 'Oh my goodness' — Jean's realization that she may have technically broken up with her daughter's boyfriend, delivered with the horror of someone who only now understands what they did
jokes.explanation: 'Oh my goodness' perfectly captures Jean in self-discovery mode — she's piecing together what she did in real time. The question 'did you break up with my boyfriend?' is a sentence that should never exist, which makes it funny. Payoff to the Jean-meddling thread.

jokes.text: Todd vomiting/having nerves before the game; Sam encounters him in the bathroom unexpectedly while wearing the Norseman head
jokes.setup: Sam is wandering in the mascot head; Todd is throwing up from pre-game nerves
jokes.punchline: The golden jock is revealed to be vomiting from anxiety — and the first person to see this is the school mascot, whose giant head makes the whole encounter absurd
jokes.explanation: Small but effective — Todd's idol gets deflated (he vomits before games) while the scene is made absurd by Sam's mascot head. Todd complimenting Sam's funky chicken while clearly having just been sick adds a layer of comedy.

jokes.text: Neal (now wearing the Norseman head) announces 'Man with a big head coming through!' and charges onto the gym floor
jokes.setup: Neal has taken over as mascot; the game has started
jokes.punchline: 'Man with a big head coming through' — Neal announces himself as if his head size is the mascot's defining characteristic and he's a traffic hazard
jokes.explanation: Neal's announcement of himself as a physical hazard is a good transition into his chaotic mascot performance. The self-narration is very Neal — he performs FOR himself as much as the crowd.

jokes.text: Cindy, furious at Sam, yells at him through the mascot head: 'Sam, if you're mad at me, this is a really obnoxious way of showing it.' — Sam is wearing the head and literally cannot communicate back
jokes.setup: Sam is acting out in the mascot costume; Cindy correctly reads this as passive aggression
jokes.punchline: The mascot head turns Sam's passive aggression into the most active non-verbal expression possible; Cindy's complaint is accurate but she can't see his face anyway
jokes.explanation: Continues the mascot-as-emotional-metaphor thread. Cindy accurately diagnosing 'obnoxious way of showing it' while Sam literally cannot show her his real expression is cleanly ironic. The mascot head is doing more dramatic work than any prop should.

jokes.text: Sam, after faking a stabbing with the Norseman sword, plays it up for the crowd: 'Oh, I stabbed myself! Oh my God, he's gonna die. Help me. I'm dying.' — the crowd loves it
jokes.setup: Neal had theorized about sword-based mascot comedy earlier; Sam — having snapped out of his funk — executes the bit
jokes.punchline: Sam's over-the-top dying performance is a callback to Neal's earlier coaching — 'it IS funny if you do it right' — and Neal was correct
jokes.explanation: Callback payoff to Neal's 'you can stab yourself, it is if you do it right' coaching. Sam discovers Neal was correct. The emotional shift — Sam going from despondent to performing with joy — makes this land as both comedy and character beat.

jokes.text: Vicki: 'You are in SO much trouble, Sam Weir.' Neal, watching: 'Uh-oh.' Sam, to Neal: 'Thank you, sir. May I have another?'
jokes.setup: Sam's sword bit has gotten him in trouble with Vicki
jokes.punchline: 'Thank you, sir. May I have another?' — a hazing/spanking reference indicating Sam is now fully committed to being funny regardless of consequences
jokes.explanation: The Animal House reference delivered by Sam in a mascot head is charming. Functions more as a character pivot moment (Sam choosing comedy over compliance) than as a standalone great joke.

jokes.text: Nick, after Lindsay finally breaks up with him, responds with: 'Good.' Then after a beat: 'Great.' Then after another beat, in a completely different tone: 'No hard feelings, right?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just broken up with Nick, who we know gets 'berserk' when dumped; we've been dreading this scene all episode
jokes.punchline: Nick's 'Good. Great.' — the painful, unconvincing performance of being fine — followed by 'no hard feelings?' which reveals his fragility
jokes.explanation: The scene subverts the episode's entire dread arc — we've been warned about Nick going berserk, told about the stalking poems, built up for an explosion, and instead we get 'Good. Great.' The comedy is structural: Nick's two-word bluff ('Good') escalating into the vulnerable 'No hard feelings?' shows exactly how much he's struggling to perform cool. The cringe is real but the subversion of expectation is sophisticated. Justifies the 8+: the joke is the resolution of the episode's main anxiety, and it delivers through misdirection and character precision rather than explicit jokes.

jokes.text: A cheerleader punches Neal (in the Norseman head) during the game chaos. Jean, watching from the stands: 'That girl just punched my little boy!'
jokes.setup: Neal is performing rogue mascot antics at the game; things have gotten physical
jokes.punchline: Jean's outraged maternal intervention from the stands — calling Neal her 'little boy' as he gets punched in a Viking helmet
jokes.explanation: Jean's instinctive mama-bear response to Neal (not her child) getting punched is sweet and funny — she's adopted him. 'My little boy' applied to a kid in a Viking helmet adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: Jean: 'That girl just punched my little boy!' — a Lincoln cheerleader has punched Neal-as-Norseman in retaliation
jokes.setup: Neal is provoking the Lincoln cheerleaders; one punches him
jokes.punchline: Jean's maternal outrage ('my little boy!') is still directed at 'Sam' — she doesn't know it's Neal getting punched
jokes.explanation: Jean's maternal protective instinct misdirected at Neal-as-Sam is a small payoff for the identity-swap joke. The physical comedy of Neal getting punched is good; Jean's horror makes it land.

jokes.text: Neal falls from the pyramid. Vicki: 'Sam, you've ruined everything!' Neal (dazed): 'Where am I? Who the hell are you?' Vicki: 'You're dead.' Neal: 'Nate Schweeber, you're a jerk!' Vicki: 'It's NEAL SCHWEIBER!'
jokes.setup: The pyramid collapses; Neal is dazed from the fall
jokes.punchline: Vicki's indignant correction of Neal's name — he got his own name wrong while being concussed — and her knowing his correct name while being furious at him
jokes.explanation: The detail of Neal getting his own name wrong while concussed — 'Nate Schweeber' — and Vicki correcting him with full fury is a perfectly absurd detail. The exchange works because Vicki's correction is delivered with the same energy as her death threat.

jokes.text: Vicki corrects herself: she called Neal 'Sam' when she was yelling at him. Neal, dazed: 'Nate Schweeber, you're a jerk.' 'It's Neal Schweiber!' — furious about the name even while concussed
jokes.setup: Neal has just fallen and is possibly concussed
jokes.punchline: Neal's priority — even while dazed — is correcting his name. 'It's Neal Schweiber!' delivered with indignation while lying on the gym floor
jokes.explanation: Neal's name-correction from a dazed, concussed state is among the most character-specific jokes in the episode. In his hierarchy of concerns, being misidentified outranks potential brain injury. 'It's Neal Schweiber!' as an outraged corrective from the gym floor is genuinely great — it tells you everything about Neal as a person. Character integration is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Bill: 'Neal, that was incredible. You're the funniest mascot I've ever seen.' Neal: 'Thanks, Bill.' Bill: 'No, no, you're a comic genius!' Neal: 'Did you see when she hit me? Wasn't that funny?'
jokes.setup: The game has ended; Neal is celebrating his mascot triumph
jokes.punchline: Neal's 'did you see when she hit me? Wasn't that funny?' — he's already reframed being punched by a Lincoln cheerleader as a bit he did, a performance choice
jokes.explanation: Neal retroactively taking credit for being punched is great character work — he has absorbed a painful, humiliating moment and reframed it as an intentional bit. This is who Neal is: every indignity becomes material.

jokes.text: Final scene: 'There he is. Get him, girls!' — the cheerleaders chase Neal. He begs: 'Come on, please! Let's talk about this!' then: 'No! Oh God! I'm a bleeder!'
jokes.setup: Neal has crashed the cheerleaders' pyramid, gotten punched, and generally caused chaos — the chickens come home to roost
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a bleeder!' — Neal's final declaration as the cheerleaders close in; it's both a comedic exit line and consistent with his hypochondriac tendencies
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing gag for Neal's storyline. 'I'm a bleeder!' is the kind of specific, character-authentic exit line that defines a character in one phrase. Neal invoking a medical condition while fleeing cheerleader vengeance is both funny and entirely consistent with his personality. High quotability — this is the line people remember from this episode. Strong escalation payoff.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay's parents forbid her from hanging out with Kim, as they see her as a bad influence. Bill grows tired of being picked last for teams for baseball in P.E. class, and not being allowed to really participate.
